title: The Contagion of Labor: Linking Workplace Copresence and Occupational Mobility Patterns
abstract: This article proposes that workplaces are key sites for career exploration and development. By bringing together workers of distinct occupations, workplaces facilitate the exchange of information and skill, generating mobility resources for individuals to transition into other occupations. We examine the connection between workplace copresence and occupational mobility patterns with two studies. First, using a novel measure of occupation-establishment copresence, we find that the frequency of two occupations sharing the same workplace is robustly linked to the prevalence of between-occupation transitions in the United States. Second, using establishment-employee matched data in France, we show that individual mobility varies by workplace staffing pattern: Those with more coworkers in certain occupations are more likely to transition into these occupations, even after they exit the firm. Together, these results indicate that workplaces play a critical role in shaping occupational mobility.
authors_text: Lin, Ken-Hou; Hung, Koit; Piccone, David; Handwerker, Elizabeth Weber; Dey, Matthew; Voldoire, Theo
